# Regression guard: an operation that records a seal obligation but leaves every
# secret's recipient set unchanged must still be discharged by a plain seal.
#
# Two variants of the same trap:
#   1. `add cv --group deploy` where deploy has no members => change_access, but
#      the recipients still resolve to admin-only, so nothing is re-encrypted.
#   2. `kill` a user who was a recipient of no secret => user.kill, but again no
#      secret's recipient set changes.
#
# In both cases Seal(false) re-encrypts nothing (rootHash unchanged), yet it must
# still emit a seal entry so SealRequiredSeqID clears and verify stops nagging.
# Before the fix only `sesam seal --seal-all` cleared it.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# --- Variant 1: change_access to an empty group ---------------------------

# Track a directory of secrets (admin-only), then reveal the plaintext.
exec sesam add cv
exec sesam open

# Change the directory's group to 'deploy', which has no members yet. This is a
# change_access whose effective recipient set does not move (still admin-only),
# so Seal(false) re-encrypts nothing - but it must still discharge the seal.
exec sesam add cv --group deploy
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"secret.change_access"'

# The seal ran as part of `add`, so there must be no pending seal.
exec sesam verify
! stderr 'a seal is pending'

# A standalone `sesam seal` is likewise a clean no-op, not a stuck obligation.
exec sesam seal
exec sesam verify
! stderr 'a seal is pending'

# --- Variant 2: killing a user with access to no secret -------------------

# Add bob, but only to a group ('ops') that no secret uses. He is a recipient of
# nothing, so killing him changes no secret's recipient set.
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --group ops
exec sesam verify
! stderr 'a seal is pending'

exec sesam kill --user bob
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"user.kill"'

# The kill recorded a seal obligation but re-encrypted nothing; the trailing
# Seal(false) must still have cleared it.
exec sesam verify
! stderr 'a seal is pending'

-- cv/a.txt --
alpha
-- cv/b.txt --
bravo
